title: Crisis Response: Acting Under Pressure When Stakes Are High
summary: In an outage, security incident, or evacuation, a Foreign Service IT officer protects life-safety and mission-critical systems first, acts on documented procedures, contains the problem, and escalates through proper channels rather than improvising alone.
content:
Crisis moments test whether you can stay calm and follow a disciplined sequence. The Foreign Service order of priorities is fixed: people first, then classified/national-security assets, then mission-critical operations, then everything else. When a crisis hits, your job is not to be a hero — it is to execute the plan that already exists and to bring the right people in fast.

• Lead with safety and security. If there is any threat to people (fire, hostile action, hazardous conditions during an evacuation), human life comes before any system or data. Only after life-safety is addressed do you weigh classified-asset protection (e.g., emergency destruction of classified material per post procedures) and mission continuity.

• Act on documented procedures, not memory or instinct. Posts maintain Emergency Action Plans (EAP), incident-response runbooks, and COOP/communications-failover procedures for a reason. In a crisis you follow the checklist; you do not invent a new process under stress. If a procedure exists, use it; if one is missing, that is a gap to fix afterward, not a license to freelance now.

• Contain, then escalate. Limit the blast radius first — isolate the affected segment, disconnect a compromised host, fail over comms to the backup path — then immediately notify the chain (supervisor, RSO/ISSO for security matters, post leadership, and Department operations centers as required). Containment buys time; escalation brings authority and resources. Never sit on a crisis hoping to fix it quietly before anyone notices.

• Restore by priority, communicate continuously. Bring mission-critical and emergency-communications capability back before convenience services. Give leadership honest, frequent status updates even when the news is bad — silence during a crisis is itself a failure.

• Document as you go and debrief after. Keep a timeline of what happened and what you did; it feeds the after-action review and any required incident report. The goal of the debrief is to improve procedures, not to assign blame.

BEST answers protect people, follow the EAP/runbook, contain before they escalate, and keep leadership informed. WEAK answers improvise solo, hide the problem, restore the easy thing first, or freeze waiting for perfect information.
keyPoints: Life-safety always outranks systems, data, and uptime — secure people first.
After people, protect classified/national-security assets per post procedures, then mission-critical operations.
Execute the documented EAP, incident runbook, or comms-failover plan — do not improvise a new process under stress.
Contain the incident (isolate, disconnect, fail over) before it spreads, then escalate immediately.
Escalate through the proper chain: supervisor, ISSO/RSO for security, post leadership, and Department ops centers as required.
Never conceal or delay reporting a crisis to look like you handled it quietly.
Restore mission-critical and emergency comms before convenience services.
Communicate honest, frequent status to leadership — even bad news, even with incomplete information.
Keep a contemporaneous timeline and complete required incident reports.
Hold a blameless after-action review to close procedure gaps for next time.

flashcards.front: A ransomware alert fires on a workstation in the political section during business hours. What is your first move?
flashcards.back: Contain first: isolate/disconnect the affected host from the network to stop spread, then immediately notify the ISSO and your supervisor and follow the incident-response runbook. Containment limits the blast radius; escalation brings the right authority. Do not try to clean it quietly.

flashcards.front: During an evacuation, classified material is still on an unattended server while staff are moving to the safe area. What governs your choices?
flashcards.back: People first — ensure personnel are safe and accounted for under the Emergency Action Plan. Emergency protection/destruction of classified assets follows post procedures and the RSO's direction, but never at the cost of a person's safety.

flashcards.front: Why follow the documented runbook instead of your own faster fix during an outage?
flashcards.back: Documented procedures are pre-vetted for safety, security, and dependencies you may not be considering under stress. Improvising can cause data loss or cascading failure. Execute the plan; fix plan gaps in the after-action review.

flashcards.front: The primary internet/VPN link fails and secure comms are affected. What sequence do you follow?
flashcards.back: Activate the documented communications-failover/COOP plan to restore emergency and mission-critical comms first, notify leadership and the Department ops center, and leave convenience services for last.

flashcards.front: You think you can fix a major outage in 20 minutes before anyone notices. Should you wait to report it?
flashcards.back: No. Escalate immediately in parallel with your fix. Concealing or delaying reporting is a weak, high-risk choice — if your fix fails you've lost time and trust. Contain, notify the chain, then work the problem with support.

flashcards.front: Mid-incident, leadership asks for a status but you don't have full answers yet. What do you tell them?
flashcards.back: Give an honest update with what is known, what is unknown, what you're doing, and when you'll report next. Frequent candid updates build trust; silence erodes it. You don't need complete information to communicate responsibly.

flashcards.front: After partial restoration, do you bring back the cafeteria kiosk or the consular/emergency-comms systems first?
flashcards.back: Mission-critical and emergency-communications systems first. Restore by mission impact, not by what's easiest or most visible.

flashcards.front: What is 'contain then escalate' and why that order?
flashcards.back: Contain = stop the bleeding (isolate the host, cut the affected segment, fail over). Escalate = notify the chain to bring authority and resources. Contain first so the problem doesn't grow while you wait for help; both happen fast and nearly together.

flashcards.front: The crisis is over. What's the most valuable thing you do next?
flashcards.back: Run a blameless after-action review with a documented timeline, complete any required incident reports, and update procedures to close the gaps you found. The purpose is learning and resilience, not blame.

flashcards.front: You're alone, the senior officer is unreachable, and a security incident is unfolding. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Follow the documented escalation tree to the next available authority (alternate ISSO/RSO, duty officer, Department ops center) and act within your documented procedures to contain. Don't invent major decisions solo — escalate to whoever is reachable.

flashcards.front: Why is freezing or waiting for perfect information a weak crisis response?
flashcards.back: In a crisis, inaction lets harm spread. The best officers take the next defined step on the runbook with the information they have — contain and escalate — then refine as more is known. Decisiveness within procedure beats paralysis.

title: Diplomatic Problem-Solving: Solving Tech Problems Within Diplomatic Boundaries
summary: An embassy IT officer solves problems while respecting host-nation sensitivities, staying inside authorization boundaries, managing vendors firmly but fairly, communicating clearly to leadership, and offering constructive solutions instead of assigning blame.
content:
Technology decisions at a post are never purely technical — they sit inside a diplomatic, legal, and political context. The same fix that is trivial at home may violate host-nation law, offend a partner, or exceed your delegated authority abroad. Diplomatic problem-solving means getting to a workable solution without creating a diplomatic problem.

• Respect host-nation sensitivity and sovereignty. Be aware of local laws on data, telecom, import of equipment, and surveillance. Coordinate with the appropriate post offices (e.g., RSO, management, and the front office) before taking actions that touch host-government systems or could be seen as overstepping. Treat Locally Employed Staff and host-nation partners with cultural respect.

• Stay inside authorization boundaries. Know what you are delegated to decide versus what requires ISSO, post leadership, contracting officer, or Department approval. Connecting new systems, signing off on spend, granting access, or deviating from standards usually requires proper authority. When in doubt, ask — overstepping is a serious failure even when well-intended.

• Manage vendors professionally. Hold vendors to contract terms (scope, security requirements, SLAs) and route changes through the contracting officer or COR rather than agreeing to side deals. Be firm on requirements and respectful in tone; the relationship is long-term and reputational.

• Communicate to leadership in their language. Translate technical issues into mission impact, options, risks, costs, and a clear recommendation. Leaders need decisions framed around what it means for the mission, not a packet of jargon. Surface bad news early and paired with proposed solutions.

• Solve constructively, not by blame. When something breaks or a colleague erred, focus on fixing the issue and preventing recurrence. Assigning blame burns relationships and rarely improves the system. Bring people together around the solution.

BEST answers respect host-nation and post equities, work within delegated authority, manage vendors through proper channels, give leadership decision-ready options, and lead with solutions. WEAK answers act unilaterally, ignore local law or post coordination, cut vendor side deals, dump jargon on leadership, or look for someone to blame.
keyPoints: Check host-nation law and sensitivities (data, telecom, equipment, surveillance) before acting; coordinate with RSO/management/front office.
Treat Locally Employed Staff and host-nation partners with cultural respect — they are essential to mission success.
Know and stay within your delegated authority; route decisions that exceed it to the proper approver.
Connecting systems, granting access, spending funds, or deviating from standards needs proper authorization.
Hold vendors to contract scope and security terms; route changes through the CO/COR, never side deals.
Frame issues to leadership as mission impact + options + risks + cost + a clear recommendation.
Surface problems and bad news early, always paired with proposed solutions.
Choose constructive problem-solving over blame; focus on the fix and prevention.
When uncertain about authority or local rules, ask before acting.
Preserve long-term relationships — tone and process matter as much as the technical outcome.

flashcards.front: The host government asks you to connect their network directly to an embassy system to 'make things easier.' What do you do?
flashcards.back: Don't agree on the spot. This touches sovereignty, security policy, and almost certainly exceeds your authority. Coordinate with the ISSO, RSO, and front office and route through proper Department channels. Find an authorized path, not an expedient one.

flashcards.front: A vendor offers to add a feature 'for free' if you skip the formal change process. Good deal?
flashcards.back: No. Side deals bypass the contracting officer/COR, contract terms, and security review, creating real liabilities. Route all changes through the CO/COR. Be appreciative but firm: scope and security go through the proper process.

flashcards.front: How should you present a serious server failure to the Ambassador?
flashcards.back: In mission terms: what's affected, the operational impact, your options with risks and costs, and a clear recommendation, then the timeline. Lead with impact and a recommended decision, not jargon. Bring bad news early and with a path forward.

flashcards.front: A standard fix at home would violate a local data-storage law. What governs your choice?
flashcards.back: Host-nation law and post policy take precedence over convenience. Find a compliant alternative and coordinate with management/RSO/legal as needed. Respecting host-nation legal sensitivities protects the mission and the relationship.

flashcards.front: A colleague's misconfiguration caused an outage. How do you handle it?
flashcards.back: Focus on restoring service and preventing recurrence, give private feedback if coaching is warranted, and frame the after-action constructively. Publicly blaming the colleague damages trust and morale and doesn't fix the system.

flashcards.front: You're unsure whether granting a requested access change is within your authority. What's the best move?
flashcards.back: Stop and verify. Check the access-approval policy and confirm with the ISSO/approving authority before acting. Overstepping authorization — even to be helpful — is a serious failure. When in doubt, ask first.

flashcards.front: Why frame tech issues around mission impact when briefing leadership?
flashcards.back: Leaders make decisions about the mission, not about protocols. Translating into impact, options, risk, and cost lets them decide quickly and correctly. Jargon-dumping abdicates your role as the translator between tech and mission.

flashcards.front: A Locally Employed Staff member proposes a workaround that conflicts with security policy but reflects local know-how. How do you respond?
flashcards.back: Respect the expertise and relationship: acknowledge the idea, explain the policy constraint, and work together toward a compliant solution. Find the authorized version of their good instinct.

flashcards.front: The front office wants something done fast that would require deviating from a Department IT standard. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Explain the standard and its purpose, lay out the risk, and seek the proper waiver/authorization rather than quietly breaking standard. If leadership wants the deviation, get it approved through the right channel and documented.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes a constructive problem-solver from a blame-shifter on the DTOT?
flashcards.back: The constructive officer convenes people around the fix, owns their part, communicates options, and respects boundaries and relationships. The blame-shifter looks for fault, acts unilaterally, and protects themselves over the mission.

title: Resource Prioritization: Triage by Mission Impact, Not by Pressure
summary: When demands compete, an embassy IT officer triages by mission impact and urgency rather than by rank, ease, or who shouts loudest, manages trade-offs transparently, and protects availability and schedule integrity through disciplined change management.
content:
There is never enough time, budget, or staff to do everything at once. The discipline of resource prioritization is deciding what to do first on a principled basis and being able to explain why. The Foreign Service standard is to serve the mission, which means impact and urgency drive the queue — not seniority, not the easiest ticket, not the loudest requester.

• Triage by mission impact and urgency. A consular outage that halts visa processing outranks a single user's printer, even if the requester is junior. Rank a request by how much it affects the mission and how time-sensitive it is. 'Important and urgent' first; 'easy and convenient' is not a priority criterion.

• Resist rank pressure on the queue. A senior official's non-critical request does not automatically jump ahead of a mission-critical issue affecting many users. Acknowledge the request respectfully, explain the prioritization, and offer a realistic timeline. Fairness and mission focus protect your credibility.

• Make trade-offs explicit. When capacity is constrained, name what gets deferred and the consequence, and let the proper authority decide if the priorities are contested. Don't silently drop work or overcommit; surface the trade-off so leadership can choose with eyes open.

• Allocate budget to mission risk and need. Spend where it reduces the most mission risk or enables the most critical capability, follow procurement rules, and document the rationale. Avoid pet projects and gold-plating when core needs are unmet.

• Balance change against availability, and protect the schedule. Don't push risky changes during peak operations or critical events; use maintenance windows and change-management process. Honor commitments and schedules; if you must slip, communicate early and renegotiate rather than quietly missing a date.

BEST answers triage by impact and urgency, hold the line against rank/convenience, make trade-offs visible, spend on mission risk, and protect availability via change control. WEAK answers serve the loudest or most senior first, chase easy wins, hide trade-offs, gold-plate, or jam in risky changes that threaten uptime and deadlines.
keyPoints: Rank work by mission impact and urgency, not by requester's rank, the ease of the task, or who complains loudest.
Mission-critical, multi-user outages outrank individual convenience requests.
When a senior official's non-urgent ask competes with a critical issue, explain the prioritization and give a realistic timeline rather than reflexively reprioritizing.
Make capacity trade-offs explicit: state what's deferred and the consequence, and escalate contested priorities to the proper authority.
Allocate budget to reduce the greatest mission risk and enable critical capability; follow procurement rules and document rationale.
Avoid gold-plating and pet projects while core mission needs are unmet.
Schedule changes for maintenance windows; avoid risky changes during peak operations or critical diplomatic events.
Use change-management process to balance improvement against availability and stability.
Protect schedule integrity: honor commitments, and if a date must slip, communicate early and renegotiate.
Don't silently drop work or overcommit — transparency lets leadership make informed priority calls.

flashcards.front: A junior officer's printer is down and, at the same time, the consular visa system fails for the whole section. Who do you help first?
flashcards.back: The consular system — far greater mission impact and urgency, affecting many users and core operations. Triage by impact and urgency, never by requester rank. Help the printer after the critical issue is contained.

flashcards.front: A senior official demands their non-urgent software install be done now, ahead of an outage you're fixing. Best response?
flashcards.back: Respectfully acknowledge, explain you're resolving a mission-critical outage, and give a realistic time for their request. Rank doesn't reorder a critical-vs-routine queue. Hold the line on mission priority, courteously.

flashcards.front: You have capacity for only two of four requested projects this quarter. How do you decide and communicate?
flashcards.back: Rank by mission impact and risk reduction, pick the top two, and explicitly tell leadership what's deferred and the consequence. If priorities are contested, let the proper authority decide. Make the trade-off visible.

flashcards.front: Why is 'it's the easiest ticket' a weak basis for prioritization?
flashcards.back: Ease is a convenience criterion, not a mission criterion. Clearing easy tickets while a high-impact issue waits hurts the mission. Rank by impact and urgency, even when the top item is hard.

flashcards.front: You have leftover budget. Spend it on a nice-to-have lab upgrade or shore up an aging system supporting consular operations?
flashcards.back: Fund the consular-supporting system — allocate budget to the greatest mission risk and critical capability, follow procurement rules, and document the rationale. Avoid gold-plating while core needs carry risk.

flashcards.front: A vendor can deploy a major upgrade during next week's high-profile diplomatic visit. Proceed?
flashcards.back: No. Avoid risky changes during peak operations or critical events. Schedule it for a maintenance window after the visit via change management. Protecting availability during critical periods outranks speed.

flashcards.front: You realize you'll miss a committed delivery date. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Communicate early, explain why, and renegotiate the schedule — don't quietly miss it. Schedule integrity means honoring commitments and surfacing slips proactively so others can adjust.

flashcards.front: Two stakeholders insist their projects are both 'top priority' and you can't do both. How do you resolve it?
flashcards.back: Assess each by mission impact/urgency, present the trade-off and your recommendation to the authority who owns both equities, and let them decide. Don't overcommit to both or pick the louder one — escalate transparently.

flashcards.front: What role does change management play in resource prioritization?
flashcards.back: It balances delivering improvements against protecting availability and stability. Changes go through review and into maintenance windows so prioritizing new work never quietly endangers uptime of mission-critical systems.

flashcards.front: Is responding fastest to the loudest complainer good prioritization?
flashcards.back: No. Volume isn't a mission metric. Triage by impact and urgency and explain your sequencing. Rewarding the loudest requester distorts the queue and shortchanges higher-impact work.

title: Interpersonal Judgment: Leading and Collaborating With Integrity
summary: An embassy IT officer coaches staff, gives feedback privately and credit publicly, owns mistakes, handles gifts and pressure ethically, and keeps loyalty to colleagues from overriding security obligations.
content:
Technical skill gets you only so far; the Foreign Service runs on relationships and trust. Interpersonal judgment is how you treat people while still upholding standards — coaching rather than scolding, sharing credit, taking responsibility, and never letting personal loyalty trump security or integrity.

• Coach and give feedback constructively and privately. Correct mistakes in private, focused on behavior and improvement, not public humiliation. Develop your staff — especially Locally Employed Staff — through mentoring. Praise and credit, by contrast, should be given publicly and generously; let the team and individuals be seen for their contributions.

• Own your mistakes. If you err, say so, fix it, and report it through proper channels. Covering up an error is far worse than the error itself and destroys trust. Accountability up and down the chain is a core value.

• Handle gifts and ethics by the rules. Decline or report gifts that exceed limits or create a conflict of interest, especially from vendors or host-nation contacts. When unsure, consult the ethics official. Perception of impropriety matters as much as actual impropriety.

• Don't let loyalty override security. If a friend or respected colleague asks you to bend a security rule — share a password, skip an access review, overlook a violation — loyalty does not justify it. Report security concerns even when it's personally uncomfortable. The obligation runs to the mission and the law, not to protecting a colleague's lapse.

• Handle pressure and rank with composure. Stay respectful but firm when a senior person pushes you toward something improper or unsafe. Your job is to give honest advice and uphold standards, not to please. Manage your own stress so it doesn't degrade your judgment or how you treat others.

BEST answers coach privately, credit publicly, own errors, follow ethics rules, and put security above personal loyalty while staying respectful under pressure. WEAK answers humiliate, take credit, hide mistakes, accept improper gifts, cover for friends, or cave to rank.
keyPoints: Give corrective feedback privately and focus on behavior and improvement, not humiliation.
Give credit publicly and generously; share recognition with the team and individuals.
Mentor and develop staff, including Locally Employed Staff, as a core responsibility.
Own mistakes openly, fix them, and report them — concealment is worse than the error.
Decline or report gifts that exceed limits or create conflicts of interest; consult the ethics official when unsure.
Avoid even the appearance of impropriety, especially with vendors and host-nation contacts.
Never let loyalty to a friend or colleague override security rules or reporting duties.
Report security concerns and violations even when personally uncomfortable.
Stay respectful but firm under pressure from senior officials; give honest advice over flattery.
Manage your own stress so it doesn't degrade your judgment or treatment of others.

flashcards.front: A team member made an embarrassing error in front of others. How and where do you correct it?
flashcards.back: In private, focused on the behavior and how to improve, not on shaming. Public correction humiliates and erodes trust. Coach privately; give any deserved praise publicly. Feedback private, credit public.

flashcards.front: A project you led succeeded largely because of a junior LE Staff member's work. Who gets the credit?
flashcards.back: Give the credit publicly to the staff member and the team. Sharing recognition builds trust and develops people; taking credit for others' work is a serious integrity failure and demoralizes staff.

flashcards.front: You realize you caused a configuration mistake that led to downtime. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Own it: acknowledge the error, fix it, and report it through proper channels. Concealing a mistake is worse than the mistake and destroys credibility. Accountability is a core Foreign Service value.

flashcards.front: A vendor offers you an expensive gift after a contract award. How do you respond?
flashcards.back: Decline it (or report it if it exceeds gift limits or creates a conflict), and consult the ethics official if unsure. Even the appearance of impropriety with a vendor compromises the mission's integrity.

flashcards.front: A close colleague asks you to share your password 'just this once' to meet a deadline. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Refuse — sharing credentials violates security policy regardless of friendship. Loyalty never overrides security obligations. Help them through a legitimate path (e.g., a proper access request) instead.

flashcards.front: You discover a respected friend committed a security violation. Do you report it?
flashcards.back: Yes. Report security concerns through proper channels even when uncomfortable. Protecting a friend by staying silent makes you complicit and endangers the mission. Integrity outranks personal loyalty.

flashcards.front: A senior official pressures you to do something you believe is unsafe or improper. How do you handle it?
flashcards.back: Stay respectful but firm: explain the risk/policy and give honest advice; if they persist with something improper, escalate or document and seek the proper authority. Don't cave to rank against your obligations.

flashcards.front: Why is hiding a mistake considered worse than the mistake itself?
flashcards.back: Mistakes are recoverable and often forgivable; concealment is a breach of integrity that destroys trust and can compound harm (delayed fixes, security exposure). The Foreign Service prizes accountability.

flashcards.front: How should you develop your Locally Employed Staff?
flashcards.back: Through active mentoring, constructive feedback, delegating growth opportunities, and public recognition. LE Staff are essential to mission continuity; investing in their development is good leadership.

flashcards.front: A colleague asks you to overlook their expired security training 'as a favor.' What do you do?
flashcards.back: Decline and follow policy — favors don't override compliance requirements. Encourage them to complete the training and, if it's a reportable lapse, handle it through proper channels.

title: Security & Compliance: Report, Don't Conceal
summary: An embassy IT officer treats security as everyone's duty — reporting phishing and incidents immediately, handling classified spills by the book, refusing unauthorized devices, conducting access reviews, and never concealing or falsifying anything.
content:
Security and compliance are non-negotiable in a Foreign Service environment that is a constant target for hostile intelligence services. The governing instinct is simple: when something goes wrong, report it immediately and follow procedure — never hide, downplay, or falsify. Reporting fast limits damage; concealment turns a manageable incident into a career-ending integrity failure.

• Report phishing and suspicious activity immediately. Don't click, don't engage, and don't just delete it quietly — report it to the ISSO/security team per policy so others can be protected and patterns can be tracked. Treat every credential prompt and unexpected attachment with suspicion.

• Handle a classified data spill by the book. If classified information lands on a system not authorized for it (a 'spill'), stop, do not forward or delete on your own, isolate the affected system, and report to the ISSO immediately so proper cleanup procedures run. Improvising — especially deleting evidence — makes it worse.

• Refuse and report unauthorized devices. Personal USB drives, unapproved phones, rogue Wi-Fi/hotspots, and shadow IT are prohibited on secure networks. Don't connect them, don't let others, and report attempts. Unauthorized devices are a primary exfiltration and malware vector.

• Conduct access reviews and least privilege. Periodically verify that accounts and permissions match current roles, remove access for departed staff promptly, and grant only what the job requires. Stale and excessive access is a top vulnerability.

• Never conceal or falsify; always report incidents. Do not alter logs, backdate records, or hide a violation — including your own. Self-report mistakes. Falsification is among the gravest offenses and compounds any underlying problem.

BEST answers report immediately, follow incident procedure, enforce least privilege and device policy, and act with total honesty — including self-reporting. WEAK answers click/engage, quietly delete or clean up spills, tolerate unauthorized devices, let access drift, or conceal/falsify to avoid trouble.
keyPoints: Report phishing and suspicious messages to the ISSO/security team immediately — don't click, engage, or just silently delete.
On a classified spill, stop, don't forward or self-delete, isolate the system, and report to the ISSO at once for proper cleanup.
Prohibit and report unauthorized devices (personal USBs, unapproved phones, rogue hotspots, shadow IT) on secure networks.
Conduct regular access reviews; enforce least privilege and remove departed-staff access promptly.
Never conceal, downplay, alter, or falsify records, logs, or incident details — falsification is among the gravest offenses.
Self-report your own security mistakes through proper channels; concealment is worse than the incident.
Report all incidents promptly so damage is contained and patterns are detected.
Verify identity and authorization before granting access or resetting credentials (guard against social engineering).
Follow data-handling and classification rules; keep classified data only on authorized systems.
Security is a shared duty — escalate concerns even about peers or seniors.

flashcards.front: You receive an email asking you to 'verify your credentials' via a link. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Don't click or enter credentials. Report it to the ISSO/security team per phishing-reporting policy rather than quietly deleting it, so others can be warned and the threat tracked.

flashcards.front: Classified information appears in an email on an unclassified system. What's the correct response?
flashcards.back: Treat it as a spill: stop, do not forward or delete it yourself, isolate the affected system, and report to the ISSO immediately so authorized cleanup runs. Self-deleting destroys evidence and worsens it.

flashcards.front: A colleague wants to use a personal USB drive on the secure network 'just quickly.' Allow it?
flashcards.back: No. Personal/unauthorized USB devices are prohibited on secure networks — a top malware and exfiltration vector. Refuse, offer an approved transfer method, and report the attempt if appropriate.

flashcards.front: Why report a phishing email instead of just deleting it?
flashcards.back: Reporting lets the security team warn others, block the sender, and detect a broader campaign. Silently deleting protects only you and leaves colleagues exposed. Reporting is the policy-compliant move.

flashcards.front: You made a security mistake that no one has noticed. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Self-report it through proper channels immediately. Concealment is worse than the mistake and is an integrity failure; prompt reporting limits damage and preserves trust.

flashcards.front: Someone asks you to adjust a log to remove evidence of a minor policy breach. Your response?
flashcards.back: Refuse absolutely. Altering or falsifying records is among the gravest offenses — far worse than the original breach. Report the request and the underlying issue. Integrity is non-negotiable.

flashcards.front: During an access review you find a departed employee's account still active with broad permissions. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Disable/remove the access promptly and report the gap. Stale accounts are a top vulnerability. Access reviews enforce least privilege — accounts must match current roles, and departures must trigger prompt deprovisioning.

flashcards.front: A caller claiming to be a senior official demands an urgent password reset. How do you proceed?
flashcards.back: Verify identity and authorization through established procedures before doing anything — urgency and claimed rank are classic social-engineering levers. Authenticate first, then act per policy.

flashcards.front: You spot an unapproved Wi-Fi hotspot operating inside the secure area. What's the right action?
flashcards.back: Report it to the ISSO/security team immediately and don't connect to it. Rogue wireless can bypass controls and enable exfiltration. Unauthorized devices must be reported, not tolerated.

flashcards.front: What is the single governing principle of security and compliance for the DTOT?
flashcards.back: Report, don't conceal. When something goes wrong, report it immediately and follow documented procedure — never hide, downplay, falsify, or self-clean. Fast honest reporting contains damage.

flashcards.front: Why enforce least privilege rather than granting broad access for convenience?
flashcards.back: Excessive access expands the attack surface and the blast radius of any compromise. Granting only what the role requires limits damage and is required by policy. Convenience never justifies over-provisioning.

flashcards.front: A respected senior colleague is bypassing a security control. Do you report it?
flashcards.back: Yes. Security is a shared duty that doesn't bend for rank or relationship. Raise the concern through proper channels even though it's uncomfortable. Tolerating a senior's violation makes you complicit.
